message.content:
Adversarially review implemented task T125 ROUND 2 (goal G8, defect D44). Return the structured verdict JSON {taskId, verdict:"approve"|"disapprove", criticism:[], questions:[], defects:[], rationale, summary} and WRITE NOTHING to the ledger.

Fetch acceptance: `mcp__ledger__fetch_item` ledger=tasks item=T125.
Branch implement/T125-r2 at commit 444455eb1c5ff408e1ab71738ce11d99a67313de, based at main 26cf5c8 (worker reset from stale base — verify merge-base==26cf5c8). Worktree: /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-a04d147e72d840a79.
Diff vs base: `git -C /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia diff 26cf5c8 444455eb1c5ff408e1ab71738ce11d99a67313de`.

CONTEXT: Round 1 (02eebafa) was RECONCILED REVISE. BOTH reviewers approved the PRODUCT fan-out (fecFlushDeadline/driveAdaptiveControllerLocked/maxEligiblePathLossLocked across m.peers; tick-gate m.fecCfg!=nil) as correct + race-clean (~380 -race runs, zero DATA RACE). The revise was TWO flaky-test-assertion fixes: FIX 1 (fable, in-scope) TestFECFlushDeadlineFansOutAcrossPeers cross-psk assertion false-failed ~1/256 (MAC-less parity garbage-decodes into KindParity) — fix: on err==nil fail only if the decoded frame semantically matches peer B's parity; FIX 2 (opus, D69) TestMultipathFECDeadlineEmitsPartialGroupParity read FECSnapshot().ParityFrames racing the async post-write increment (~2%) — fix: bounded-retry poll.

Round-2 focus (this round is TEST-ONLY hardening — no product change): (1) confirm the PRODUCT diff (multipath.go, adaptive_test.go call sites) is BYTE-IDENTICAL to the round-1-approved fan-out (`git -C /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia diff 02eebafa 444455eb -- internal/bind/multipath.go internal/bind/adaptive_test.go` should be empty); (2) FIX 1 — the cross-psk assertion now requires a SEMANTIC field match (frame.Parity with peer B's Payload/DataCount/group), so an accidental garbage nil-err decode no longer false-fails, AND it still DISCRIMINATES a real isolation break (worker claims pre-fix product fails earlier at the recvParity timeout — verify the assertion isn't now vacuous); (3) FIX 2 — the bounded-retry poll on ParityFrames is correct and doesn't mask a real under-count; (4) DETERMINISM — run `nix develop -c sh -c 'go test -race -count=50 -run "FEC|Flush|Deadline|Adaptive" ./internal/bind/...'` and confirm ZERO failures across all 50. Run also `cd /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-a04d147e72d840a79 && nix develop -c sh -c 'go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./internal/bind/...' && nix develop -c just lint`. If the product is unchanged, both hardenings are correct+non-vacuous, and 50 runs are clean, verdict approve. Split criticism[] vs questions[]; file genuine defects.

attachment.content:
- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

{"item":{"id":"T125","milestoneId":"M42","status":"planned","fields":{"headline":"Fan the FEC deadline flush + adaptive-controller drive across all bound peers (D44)","description":"fecFlushDeadline (internal/bind/multipath.go ~:1962-2022) and driveAdaptiveControllerLocked (~:2039-2057) reach fecSend/scheduler/paths through the embedded-primary promotion, and encodeParityLocked is called with m.peerState hard-coded (~:2005) — so only the PRIMARY peer's straggler FEC groups get deadline parity; a non-primary peer's partial groups close only on fill, silently losing straggler parity (D44). Fix: make the flush iterate m.peers under the same TryLock, per peer Load fecSend (nil-skip a torn-down/uninstantiated peer), drive that peer's adaptive controller, Tick that peer's encoder, Pick on that peer's scheduler over that peer's paths, and frame parity with encodeParityLocked(peer, ...); accumulate wire writes outside the lock as today. ALSO fix the tick-loop START condition in Open (~:941 `m.fecSend.Load() != nil`, primary-only): start fecTickLoop whenever m.fecCfg != nil, so a concentrator peer whose fecSend materializes lazily on re-bind (ensurePeerReceiveInstantiated) still receives deadline flushes. content.text: FIX 2 poll looks correct (final assertion is exact equality, so it masks neither under- nor over-count). Now the key FIX 1 — the new multipeer test with the cross-psk assertion. Let me read it.

content.content:
1	package bind
2	
3	import (
4		"bytes"
5		"io"
6		"net"
7		"testing"
8		"time"
9	
10		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/fec"
11		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/frame"
12		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
13		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/sched"
14		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
15	)
16	
17	// newAlwaysUpScheduler builds a trivial single-path AlwaysUp active-backup scheduler for a
18	// concentrator peer fixture (D44 fan-out test): Pick always resolves to the peer's own sole
19	// path without requiring a real prober/liveness bring-up.
20	func newAlwaysUpScheduler(t testing.TB) sched.Scheduler {
21		t.Helper()
22		lg, err := log.New("error", io.Discard)
23		if err != nil {
24			t.Fatalf("build logger: %v", err)
25		}
26		scheduler, err := sched.NewActiveBackup([]sched.PathHealth{sched.AlwaysUp{}}, sched.Config{FailbackAfter: time.Second}, telemetry.SystemClock{}, lg)
27		if err != nil {
28			t.Fatalf("build scheduler: %v", err)
29		}
30		return scheduler
31	}
32	
33	// recvParity reads exactly one datagram from conn (a raw peer socket standing in for a bound
34	// peer's remote), decodes it under codec, and asserts it is a frame.Parity with the given
35	// DataCount. It returns the decoded frame plus the raw bytes, so a caller can additionally
36	// attempt (and expect to fail) decoding the same raw bytes under a DIFFERENT peer's codec —
37	// the cross-psk isolation check.
38	func recvParity(t testing.TB, conn *net.UDPConn, codec *frame.Codec, wantDataCount int) (frame.Parity, []byte) {
39		t.Helper()
40		if err := conn.SetReadDeadline(time.Now().Add(500 * time.Millisecond)); err != nil {
41			t.Fatalf("set read deadline: %v", err)
42		}
43		buf := make([]byte, maxDatagram)
44		n, err := conn.Read(buf)
45		if err != nil {
46			t.Fatalf("read parity: %v", err)
47		}
48		raw := append([]byte(nil), buf[:n]...)
49		fr, err := codec.Decode(raw)
50		if err != nil {
51			t.Fatalf("decode parity: %v", err)
52		}
53		par, ok := fr.(frame.Parity)
54		if !ok {
55			t.Fatalf("frame type = %T, want frame.Parity", fr)
56		}
57		if int(par.DataCount) != wantDataCount {
58			t.Fatalf("parity DataCount = %d, want %d", par.DataCount, wantDataCount)
59		}
60		return par, raw
61	}
62	
63	// TestFECFlushDeadlineFansOutAcrossPeers is the D44 regression: fecFlushDeadline must close and
64	// flush EVERY bound peer's OWN partial FEC group on the deadline tick, not just the embedded
65	// primary's (which is all it reached, via the primary-only promotion, before the fix). Two
66	// concentrator peers (A, the primary, and B) each admit a PARTIAL group (fewer than
67	// DataShards); one fecFlushDeadline() call must emit parity for BOTH — each decodable ONLY
68	// under its own peer's psk-derived codec — while a THIRD, torn-down peer (C, also primed with a
69	// partial group before teardown) is nil-skipped without disturbing A's or B's flush.
70	//
71	// On pre-fix code this fails at the peer-B assertion: fecFlushDeadline read only
72	// m.fecSend/m.scheduler/m.paths (the embedded primary, via promotion), so peer B's partial
73	// group received no parity on this tick and would have had to wait for a full size-triggered
74	// close — the straggler-parity loss D44 describes.
75	func TestFECFlushDeadlineFansOutAcrossPeers(t *testing.T) {
76		const dataShards, parityShards = 4, 1
77		fecCfg := &fec.Config{DataShards: dataShards, ParityShards: parityShards, Deadline: testFECDeadline}
78	
79		pskA := testKey(t, 0x61)
80		pskB := testKey(t, 0x62)
81		pskC := testKey(t, 0x63)
82	
83		m := newMultipathFEC(t, loopbackPaths(1), pskA, fecCfg)
84	
85		if err := m.AddConcentratorPeer("peer-b", pskB, newAlwaysUpScheduler(t), nil, nil); err != nil {
86			t.Fatalf("AddConcentratorPeer B: %v", err)
87		}
88		if err := m.AddConcentratorPeer("peer-c", pskC, newAlwaysUpScheduler(t), nil, nil); err != nil {
89			t.Fatalf("AddConcentratorPeer C: %v", err)
90		}
91	
92		if _, _, err := m.Open(0); err != nil {
93			t.Fatalf("Open: %v", err)
94		}
95		t.Cleanup(func() { _ = m.Close() })
96	
97		peerA := m.peerState
98		peerB := m.peersByName["peer-b"]
99		peerC := m.peersByName["peer-c"]
100	
101		rawA, rawAAddr := rawPeer(t)
102		rawB, rawBAddr := rawPeer(t)
103		rawC, rawCAddr := rawPeer(t)
104	
105		peerPathByName(peerA, "a").setRemote(rawAAddr)
106		peerPathByName(peerB, "a").setRemote(rawBAddr)
107		peerPathByName(peerC, "a").setRemote(rawCAddr)
108	
109		// Admit a PARTIAL group (< dataShards) into each peer's own encoder, directly under m.mu —
110		// the encoder's single-writer discipline — exactly as Send does, but short of a full batch
111		// so the group stays open for the deadline tick to flush.
112		admitPartial := func(peer *peerState, n int, tag byte) {
113			t.Helper()
114			fs := peer.fecSend.Load()
115			if fs == nil {
116				t.Fatalf("peer %q has no FEC send plane", peer.name)
117			}
118			m.mu.Lock()
119			defer m.mu.Unlock()
120			for i := 0; i < n; i++ {
121				_, par, err := fs.enc.Admit([]byte{tag, byte(i)})
122				if err != nil {
123					t.Fatalf("admit peer %q shard %d: %v", peer.name, i, err)
124				}
125				if par != nil {
126					t.Fatalf("peer %q group closed early (admitted %d < %d dataShards)", peer.name, n, dataShards)
127				}
128			}
129		}
130		admitPartial(peerA, dataShards-1, 0xA0)
131		admitPartial(peerB, dataShards-1, 0xB0)
132		admitPartial(peerC, dataShards-1, 0xC0)
133	
134		// Tear peer C down BEFORE the tick: teardownPeerLocked nils its fecSend (it is never "live"
135		// in this fixture — no per-path prober attached), so the tick must skip it cleanly.
136		if !m.TearDownPeer("peer-c") {
137			t.Fatal("TearDownPeer refused to tear down peer C")
138		}
139		if peerC.fecSend.Load() != nil {
140			t.Fatal("peer C's FEC send plane survived teardown")
141		}
142	
143		// The encoder's grouping deadline runs off fec.SystemClock{} (real wall time, not the
144		// bind's fake test clocks elsewhere), so a real sleep past it is required before the flush.
145		time.Sleep(testFECDeadline + 20*time.Millisecond)
146		m.fecFlushDeadline()
147	
148		codecA, err := frame.NewCodec(pskA)
149		if err != nil {
150			t.Fatalf("codec A: %v", err)
151		}
152		codecB, err := frame.NewCodec(pskB)
153		if err != nil {
154			t.Fatalf("codec B: %v", err)
155		}
156	
157		// Peer A (primary) and peer B each got their own partial group flushed on this one tick.
158		parA, _ := recvParity(t, rawA, codecA, dataShards-1)
159		parB, rawBBytes := recvParity(t, rawB, codecB, dataShards-1)
160	
161		if parA.PathID != peerPathByName(peerA, "a").id {
162			t.Fatalf("peer A parity PathID = %d, want %d", parA.PathID, peerPathByName(peerA, "a").id)
163		}
164		if parB.PathID != peerPathByName(peerB, "a").id {
165			t.Fatalf("peer B parity PathID = %d, want %d", parB.PathID, peerPathByName(peerB, "a").id)
166		}
167	
168		// Cross-psk isolation (T84): peer B's parity must NOT decode under peer A's codec into a
169		// frame matching peer B's actual emitted parity. Parity frames carry NO MAC by design
170		// (internal/frame/frame.go), so codecA.Decode(rawBBytes) can occasionally garble the kind
171		// byte to a value that lands on KindParity (~1/256 of the time) and parses without error —
172		// that accidental garbage-decode is the designed behaviour of an unauthenticated frame
173		// kind, not a cross-psk isolation violation. Only a decode that lands on a frame.Parity
174		// whose semantic fields (group/index/count/path/payload) actually MATCH peer B's real
175		// parity would demonstrate that codec A can read peer B's traffic — see perpsk_test.go:57
176		// for the analogous "err==nil but not the same content" idiom.
177		if frB, err := codecA.Decode(rawBBytes); err == nil {
178			if got, ok := frB.(frame.Parity); ok &&
179				got.FECGroup == parB.FECGroup &&
180				got.ParityIndex == parB.ParityIndex &&
181				got.DataCount == parB.DataCount &&
182				got.PathID == parB.PathID &&
183				bytes.Equal(got.Payload, parB.Payload) {
184				t.Fatal("peer B's parity decoded under peer A's codec as a matching frame.Parity — cross-psk isolation violated")
185			}
186		}
187	
188		// The torn-down peer C is skipped WITHOUT disturbing the others: its own remote never
189		// receives anything for this tick.
190		if err := rawC.SetReadDeadline(time.Now().Add(150 * time.Millisecond)); err != nil {
191			t.Fatalf("set read deadline: %v", err)
192		}
193		buf := make([]byte, maxDatagram)
194		if n, _, err := rawC.ReadFromUDP(buf); err == nil {
195			t.Fatalf("torn-down peer C unexpectedly received a %d-byte frame — teardown was not respected by the flush", n)
196		}
197	}
198

content.text: The product diff is byte-identical and FIX 2 is sound. The crux is whether FIX 1's semantic-match assertion is non-vacuous. I need to understand how `frame.Codec` transforms parity frames — whether a real cross-psk break would actually produce a field-matching `frame.Parity`.

content.text: Now I understand the crypto: parity bodies are XOR-obfuscated under a PSK-derived XChaCha20 keystream (nonce is clear). A real cross-psk break (obfKey_A == obfKey_B) yields the exact plaintext → all fields incl. Payload match parB; a wrong-key de-obfuscation yields random garbage → fields won't match. So the semantic-match assertion fires iff a real isolation break — non-vacuous. Let me confirm the round-1→round-2 test delta is ONLY the two flaky fixes, and check the whole diff scope.

input.command: echo "=== r1->r2 delta on new multipeer test (should be ONLY the cross-psk assertion FIX 1) ===" && git diff 02eebafa 444455eb -- internal/bind/fec_multipeer_flush_test.go && echo "=== r1->r2 delta whole tree (only test files) ===" && git diff --stat 02eebafa 444455eb
input.description: Diff round1 to round2 for scope confirmation

content.content:
=== r1->r2 delta on new multipeer test (should be ONLY the cross-psk assertion FIX 1) ===
diff --git a/internal/bind/fec_multipeer_flush_test.go b/internal/bind/fec_multipeer_flush_test.go
index 9767da4..1038719 100644
--- a/internal/bind/fec_multipeer_flush_test.go
+++ b/internal/bind/fec_multipeer_flush_test.go
@@ -1,6 +1,7 @@
 package bind
 
 import (
+	"bytes"
 	"io"
 	"net"
 	"testing"
@@ -164,9 +165,24 @@ func TestFECFlushDeadlineFansOutAcrossPeers(t *testing.T) {
 		t.Fatalf("peer B parity PathID = %d, want %d", parB.PathID, peerPathByName(peerB, "a").id)
 	}
 
-	// Cross-psk isolation (T84): peer B's parity must NOT decode under peer A's codec.
-	if _, err := codecA.Decode(rawBBytes); err == nil {
-		t.Fatal("peer B's parity decoded under peer A's codec — cross-psk isolation violated")
+	// Cross-psk isolation (T84): peer B's parity must NOT decode under peer A's codec into a
+	// frame matching peer B's actual emitted parity. Parity frames carry NO MAC by design
+	// (internal/frame/frame.go), so codecA.Decode(rawBBytes) can occasionally garble the kind
+	// byte to a value that lands on KindParity (~1/256 of the time) and parses without error —
+	// that accidental garbage-decode is the designed behaviour of an unauthenticated frame
+	// kind, not a cross-psk isolation violation. Only a decode that lands on a frame.Parity
+	// whose semantic fields (group/index/count/path/payload) actually MATCH peer B's real
+	// parity would demonstrate that codec A can read peer B's traffic — see perpsk_test.go:57
+	// for the analogous "err==nil but not the same content" idiom.
+	if frB, err := codecA.Decode(rawBBytes); err == nil {
+		if got, ok := frB.(frame.Parity); ok &&
+			got.FECGroup == parB.FECGroup &&
+			got.ParityIndex == parB.ParityIndex &&
+			got.DataCount == parB.DataCount &&
+			got.PathID == parB.PathID &&
+			bytes.Equal(got.Payload, parB.Payload) {
+			t.Fatal("peer B's parity decoded under peer A's codec as a matching frame.Parity — cross-psk isolation violated")
+		}
 	}
 
 	// The torn-down peer C is skipped WITHOUT disturbing the others: its own remote never

content.text:
The r1→r2 whole-tree diff includes main-advancement work (device.go/session.go/peerteardown) that landed between the two stale bases — irrelevant. The actual task diff vs base 26cf5c8 is clean: 4 in-scope files only, no ledger writes. FIX 1 delta is exactly the cross-psk assertion refinement.

Now the determinism gate and full check. Let me run the 50x -race suite plus build/vet/test/lint.

content.text:
All gates green. Let me summarize the evidence.

Evidence collected:
- **merge-base == 26cf5c8** confirmed (worker correctly reset from stale base).
- **Product unchanged**: `git diff 02eebafa 444455eb -- internal/bind/multipath.go internal/bind/adaptive_test.go` is empty — byte-identical to the round-1-approved fan-out.
- **Task diff scope clean**: vs base 26cf5c8 only 4 in-scope files (multipath.go, adaptive_test.go, fec_test.go, fec_multipeer_flush_test.go); no ledger writes in the branch.
- **FIX 1 (cross-psk assertion) non-vacuous**: parity bodies are XOR-obfuscated under a PSK-derived XChaCha20 keystream (clear nonce). A real isolation break (obfKey_A==obfKey_B) de-obfuscates to the exact plaintext → every field incl. `Payload` matches `parB`, firing the `t.Fatal`. A wrong-key decode yields random bytes → the ~1/256 accidental KindParity landing produces garbage fields that will not match, so it no longer false-fails. The assertion fires iff a genuine break. The primary D44 discriminator (peer-B `recvParity` timeout on pre-fix) is unchanged and was validated in round 1.
- **FIX 2 (bounded-retry poll) non-masking**: loop breaks on `ParityFrames >= parityShards` or a 200ms deadline; the final check is exact equality (`!= parityShards`), so an under-count times out and fails and an over-count fails — neither is masked.
- **Determinism**: `go test -race -count=50 -run "FEC|Flush|Deadline|Adaptive"` → `ok`, zero failures, no DATA RACE (174s).
- **Full check green**: `go build`, `go vet`, `go test ./internal/bind/...`, `just lint` (golangci-lint, 0 issues across all tag sets).

### Session summary
- **Did:** reviewed task T125 round 2 (diff 26cf5c8..444455eb), a test-only hardening round over the round-1-approved product fan-out
- **Achieved:** verdict approve; 0 criticisms / 0 questions / 0 defects
- **Discovered:** product diff byte-identical to r1; FIX 1 cross-psk assertion is non-vacuous (obfuscation is PSK-keyed, so a real break yields field-exact match while garbage decodes don't); FIX 2 poll masks neither under- nor over-count; 50x -race clean; build/vet/test/lint all green
- **Issues:** none

Both test-only fixes are sound: FIX 1's semantic-match cross-psk assertion is non-vacuous — parity bodies are PSK-keyed XChaCha20-obfuscated, so a real isolation break de-obfuscates to field-exact parB (fires t.Fatal) while a garbage nil-err decode yields non-matching fields; FIX 2's bounded poll asserts exact equality so it masks neither under- nor over-count. 50x -race clean (no DATA RACE); go build/vet/test and just lint all green.",
